label: Backup procedures
brief:
The backup question has two parts: what to back up, and where. The "what" is bigger than people often realize. For a single-sig wallet, you need the seed phrase plus the BIP39 passphrase (if you used one) plus the derivation path or descriptor (if your wallet uses non-default conventions). For a multisig, you also need the descriptor itself — the script template that says which keys, in what order — because without it the keys alone cannot reconstruct the wallet [1]. "I have my seed words" is necessary but not always sufficient.

The "where" is about durability and secrecy at the same time. A single paper copy in your house defeats a fire or flood; multiple copies in unrelated geographic locations defeat both. Metal seed-storage products solve the durability half (there are independent stress-test reports comparing them on heat, corrosion, and crushing) but a metal plate stamped with your seed is still readable to anyone who finds it, so storage location matters as much as media [2]. Routine practice: at least two physical copies, in physically separate secure locations, one of them in a form (metal, etched) that survives a house fire.

The failure mode that matters most and gets tested least is recovery rehearsal. Before any meaningful funds go in, sweep the seed into a fresh wallet on a separate device and verify it produces the same first receive address as the original wallet — that's the cheapest, most informative test of "my backup actually works" [3]. Six months later, do it again from the metal copy you've never read since stamping it. The realistic outcome of skipping the rehearsal: years from now, somebody discovers that one of the words was illegible, or the passphrase is misremembered, or a non-standard derivation path was used, and the funds are gone. Backup procedures fail silently. Recovery rehearsal is what makes them fail loudly, while you can still fix them.

sources.label: Bitcoin Optech — Topic: Backup
sources.excerpt: A complete wallet backup includes the cryptographic key material (seed or seeds, passphrase if any) and the wallet structure metadata (derivation paths, output descriptors, multisig configuration). For non-default scripts or multisig wallets, the descriptor is required for recovery and must be backed up alongside the key material.

sources.label: Jameson Lopp — Metal Bitcoin Seed Storage Stress Test
sources.excerpt: Independent fire, corrosion, and crush testing of commercially-available metal seed-storage products. Results vary substantially by construction method (stamped plates vs. punched tiles vs. engraved). The report is updated periodically and is the closest thing to an objective comparison of physical-medium durability for seed phrases.

sources.label: Bitcoin Wiki — Recovery best practices
sources.excerpt: Test recovery on a separate device with a small amount before funding the wallet. Repeat from the long-term backup medium at least once after creation, to verify the medium is readable as expected. Backup procedures fail silently in the absence of rehearsal; rehearsal is what surfaces problems while there is still time to correct them.
